title: Incentive Spirometry and Postoperative Breathing Exercises
subtitle: The nurse coaches lung expansion to prevent atelectasis — positioning upright, teaching a slow deep inhalation with a breath hold, splinting the incision to cough, and using the spirometer about 10 times per hour.

patient.name: Incentive Spirometry
patient.admitDate: The nurse teaches incentive spirometry and deep-breathing exercises to prevent postoperative atelectasis.

vitals.temp: Splint with a pillow to cough

nursesNote: The nurse teaches incentive spirometry and postoperative breathing exercises to prevent atelectasis and pneumonia. After surgery, shallow breathing from pain and immobility lets alveoli collapse, so deep lung expansion is encouraged. The patient sits upright in high-Fowler's, exhales normally, then seals the lips tightly around the mouthpiece and inhales slowly and deeply to raise the piston to the goal marker, holds the breath for about 3-5 seconds, and exhales. This is repeated about 10 times each hour while awake. The patient is taught to cough afterward to clear secretions and to splint the incision with a pillow to reduce pain. A common error is blowing out forcefully into the device or taking quick shallow breaths; the device is for slow, sustained inhalation. The spirometer does not replace turning and early ambulation. The nurse reports fever, crackles or decreased breath sounds, a productive cough, or a falling oxygen saturation, which can signal atelectasis or pneumonia.
providerOrders: Position the patient upright for breathing exercises
Teach incentive spirometry: slow deep inhalation with a 3-5 second hold
Use the spirometer about 10 times per hour while awake
Splint the incision and cough to clear secretions
Encourage turning and early ambulation; report signs of atelectasis

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about incentive spirometry?

opts.text: The spirometer prevents atelectasis by expanding the lungs
The patient inhales slowly and deeply through the mouthpiece
Sitting upright gives the best lung expansion
The patient should blow forcefully out into the device
Deep breathing and coughing help clear secretions
The spirometer replaces the need to ambulate
A slow deep inhalation raises and holds the piston at the goal
Holding the breath keeps the alveoli open
Splinting the incision reduces pain with coughing
Fast, shallow breaths expand the lungs just as well
Untreated atelectasis can progress to pneumonia
The device works best used only once a day
Sit the patient upright in high-Fowler's
Have the patient exhale, then seal lips on the mouthpiece
Coach a slow, deep inhalation to raise the piston
Hold the breath 3-5 seconds, then exhale
Encourage coughing and repeat ~10 times per hour
Sitting upright before using the device
Inhaling slowly and deeply through the mouthpiece
Blowing out hard into the spirometer
Holding the breath 3-5 seconds at the top
Taking quick shallow breaths to move the piston
Splinting the incision with a pillow to cough
Using it only once before sleep
Coughing to clear secretions after deep breaths

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Postop patient: shallow breaths, low-grade fever, diminished bases
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Position the patient upright
Coach incentive spirometry and deep breathing with splinting
Encourage coughing to clear secretions
Assist with ambulation per orders and encourage fluids
Reassess breath sounds and SpO2 and document
Coaching slow deep breaths with a 3-5 second hold
Telling the patient to exhale forcefully into the device
Encouraging use ~10 times per hour while awake
Skipping the spirometer because coughing hurts
Splinting the incision with a pillow to cough
Using quick shallow breaths to raise the piston
Sitting the patient upright before each session
Having the patient lie flat while using the spirometer
